title: Will The AI Boom Prove Inflationary Or Deflationary?
gist: The AI infrastructure boom is conventionally seen as inflationary early on (heavy capex, high expectations) then deflationary as productivity gains arrive; the crux for investors is timing when the cycle flips, an issue now elevated by the Fed launching task forces on AI's effect on productivity, employment and inflation.

insights.claim: The conventional framework holds that an AI infrastructure boom is inflationary in its early stages, driven by heavy capital spending and high expectations, before turning deflationary as productivity gains materialize.
insights.speaker: Will Denyer, Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: Frames AI as a two-phase macro regime; positioning should differ depending on which phase the economy is in, so investors need to track the inflection rather than assume AI is uniformly disinflationary.
insights.quote: The conventional view is that it is inflationary in its early stages, driven by heavy capital spending and high expectations, before becoming deflationary as productivity gains materialize.
insights.locator: A key question for investors and policymakers

insights.claim: The decisive investment question is timing: when the productivity gains from AI will begin to outweigh the inflationary forces created by the investment boom itself.
insights.speaker: Will Denyer, Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: Misjudging the transition point risks mispricing rates and duration; watch productivity data for the signal that the deflationary phase is arriving.
insights.quote: when those gains will begin to outweigh the inflationary forces created by the investment boom itself

insights.claim: Federal Reserve chair Kevin Warsh has launched a series of task forces to examine issues shaping future monetary policy, including AI's impact on productivity, employment and inflation.
insights.speaker: Will Denyer, Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: Signals the Fed is institutionally reconsidering its inflation and rate framework around AI; task-force conclusions could reshape the rate path and are a policy catalyst to monitor.
insights.quote: Federal Reserve chair Kevin Warsh’s decision to launch a series of task forces to examine issues shaping the future conduct of monetary policy

insights.claim: If AI materially raises the economy's productive capacity, it could permit stronger growth with less inflationary pressure, fundamentally changing how policymakers think about interest rates.
insights.speaker: Will Denyer, Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: A higher productive-capacity regime would raise the non-inflationary growth ceiling, a bullish setup for risk assets and potentially lower-for-longer real rates if the thesis holds.
insights.quote: it could allow stronger growth with less inflationary pressure, fundamentally changing how policymakers think about interest rates
insights.locator: If AI materially raises the economy’s productive capacity
